The detour costs the better part of an hour you can barely spare, but the party comes back with every hazard flagged and the safe footing carefully read. Yellow steam hangs waist-high and blinds the whole column, and the scout marks the worst of it for the column behind. The scout falls back into the line exactly where the safe road makes the waypoint ahead.

nodes.body: You push the spent party on across the sucking mud, mud that grips a boot and refuses to give it back. The danger climbs with each step you take from the safe line you abandoned behind you. The danger here is vents that can flash to steam without any warning, and retreat solves nothing out here, so you keep going.

The line grinds on into the yellow ground, mud that grips a boot and refuses to give it back. The ground ahead narrows steadily to a single bad option and no other; the danger on this line is vents that can flash to steam without any warning. Hot ooze closing warm around your ankles drags at the exhausted line, and you are too deep and too spent to do anything now but go through. There is nothing to gain by waiting, so you commit to the crossing.

The ground drops out where an old lava tube crosses the route on the yellow ground, and the line ahead falls in on itself. Nobody is caught in the collapse, which is the single mercy in the whole grinding hour. With the way sealed you retreat over the ground you already know, the valley walled off behind tons of stone. Your people are alive, at least, and that will have to be enough for now, and the valley stays exactly where it was, a green rumour on the far side of the ash.

The danger on this line is a wrong step that drops a leg thigh-deep in scalding mud. The caldera's wind funnels straight into the labouring column as it works the exposed reach of the fumarole field. There is no standing upright against it, let alone crossing the open height in it. You drive the party down off the high ground to shelter, the crossing abandoned somewhere above you.

The line grinds forward over the fumarole field, yellow crust breaking into scalding slurry underfoot. Every stride carries the column further from the sound line you left, and the ground forgives less the further you go. The danger here is fumes thick enough to gutter the lanterns to nothing; sulfur stings your eyes and coats your throat with rot. The guide warns of a wrong step that drops a leg thigh-deep in scalding mud, and there is a valley at the end of this, you remind them, and keep going.

The party works deeper into the fumarole field, yellow crust breaking into scalding slurry underfoot; the ground ahead narrows steadily to a single bad option and no other. The crossing means facing vents that can flash to steam without any warning, and wet ground trembling soft and treacherous below drags at the exhausted line. There is no turning back now with the stores this low and the safe line a full day behind. You gather the column, steady it with a word, and commit to the crossing.

The casks fail one by one across the sulfur flats, seam splitting after seam, until the last of the water is simply gone. You ration the warm dregs and the dregs do not begin to reach the valley. With nothing at all left to drink, you order the retreat before the heat can order it for you. You will be counting the cost of this hour for a long time to come; the valley stays exactly where it was, a green rumour on the far side of the ash.

The waypoint in the sulfur marsh rises out of the murk, and the column staggers gratefully into it, and sulfur hangs heavy and yellow over the sodden ground. The safe line will get them there if the stores hold; the fast lines might get them there sooner, or not at all. Every shortcut across this wasteland has already buried someone who trusted it. The call is yours, the proven line or one of the faster gambles the guide keeps naming.

Each mile off the known route trades a little more certainty for a little more speed, and the guide warns of a wrong branch that loses the column for hours in the dark. Heat-cracked stone ticking and settling as it cools slows every mile to a crawl; you set your jaw against the doubt and keep going.

Dusk closes over the cracked galleries faster than the exhausted column can cross it; the guide lays out two lines from here and swears that neither is survivable for the whole party. nodes.body: The cracked galleries breathes heat like an open forge, and the labouring column wilts and staggers in it. You push the party as far as flesh will allow and find that far is nowhere near far enough. Beaten back by the heat, you retreat to cooler ground and watch the valley recede again, and the caldera does not care in the least that you tried.

A hard tremor runs through the tunnels and throws half the column clean off its feet. By the time the ground stills again the party is scattered wide across broken, treacherous footing. You spend the last of the light gathering them in, all of them found, none of them whole in nerve, and you retreat. The wasteland keeps whatever it manages to take, and it has taken a great deal, and your people are alive, at least, and that will have to be enough for now.

The line grinds forward over the black passages, galleries branching and doubling back in the dark. You are well off the safe line now, and it tells plainly in every wary, testing footfall. You have not yet cleared a wrong branch that loses the column for hours in the dark; the only honest answer is forward, and you keep going.

The party works deeper into the black passages, passages so low the tall are forced to stoop. The party closes up tight, warm stone breathes against your face like a sleeping animal; you are too deep and too spent to do anything now but go through. You set the guide at the front, take the rear yourself, and commit to the crossing.

The hollow floods as the sky above the tunnels goes black with driven ash, and there is no holding the low line against it. You count the party out onto the last dry ground, all present and all beaten, then turn them back the way they came. The wind screams unbroken across the naked rock; there is nothing to do now but keep going.

The light fails across the scoured ridge and the guide brings the column to a wary stop, and the guide offers a choice of two poor lines and will make no promise on either. One line runs low through a hollow that traps water, the other rides an exposed rim in a rising, dangerous wind. Low line or high line, and the whole column waits in the dark on your word.

choices.label: Take the low line through the hollow.
Take the high line along the rim.

nodes.body: The crossing at the naked spine stalls, and out here a stalled column is a starving one before long. The final ration goes round and comes up short by fully half the party. You cannot drive hungry people forward on nothing, so you turn them around and the valley stays a rumour beyond the ash.

Driven off the caldera rim on every side by ground you cannot beat, you make the hardest call of the crossing. The whole expedition falls back toward base camp, mile by bitter mile, and you brought them out only to bring them home again. The valley is not lost, you tell them, only postponed, and the caldera does not care in the least that you tried. There will be other lines, you tell yourself, if there is ever time to find them.

One more relentless stretch of the naked spine passes underfoot, an open saddle where a gust can knock a man flat. The further the party strays from the proven line, the higher the stakes climb on every step. You weigh the column against an exposed spine with no shelter if the mountain shifts; turning back would cost more than pressing on, so you keep going.

The line grinds on into the naked spine, bare rock scoured clean of ash by a ceaseless wind; every branch but one has already closed itself against the column. What stands between you and open ground is an exposed spine with no shelter if the mountain shifts. Gusts arriving in blows that stagger the whole line drags at the exhausted line; you are too deep and too spent to do anything now but go through. You gather the column, steady it with a word, and commit to the crossing.

Cinder haze closes over the naked spine and the way forward simply ceases to exist in front of you. Hour after blind hour you grope for a line that is no longer there to be found. Beaten flat by the murk, you turn the party around and retreat, and there will be other lines, you tell yourself, if there is ever time to find them.

You drive them another hard mile into the caldera rim, gusts arriving in blows that stagger the whole line. The column reads the change in your face and matches it, quieter and warier with every yard. The real threat on this line is wind strong enough to take a laden pack off a shoulder. The guide sets the pace and the column keeps it, the caldera glows a sullen, restless orange far below your boots; you set your jaw against the doubt and keep going.

Yet another sunless stretch of the naked spine passes underfoot, ground offering no cover from the mountain's breath. Every stride carries the column further from the sound line you left, and the ground forgives less the further you go. The ground ahead promises an exposure that turns any misstep into a long fall, and you count the party once more and keep going.

You press on where the scoured ridge closes tighter around the column, gusts arriving in blows that stagger the whole line. Every branch but one has already closed itself against the column; the guide warns of a caldera breathing heat and fume straight up the slope. The guide reads wind strong enough to take a laden pack off a shoulder and says nothing you want to hear. You have spent far too much ground to wheel the column around at this hour; you set the guide at the front, take the rear yourself, and commit to the crossing.

The party plants itself at the high saddle and flatly refuses the crossing ahead; you see the refusal settle in their faces before anyone speaks a word of it, and you cannot honestly blame them. There is no leading people who will not follow, so you lead them home instead. The valley stays exactly where it was, a green rumour on the far side of the ash; the caldera does not care in the least that you tried.

Boots are failing and shoulders are raw, and the column has less left in it than it will admit. You weigh the safe line, certain but punishing, against the faster ground the guide keeps eyeing with open hunger. The riskier the ground, the more it demands of a column already scraped this close to empty.

The guide sets the pace and the column keeps it, living colour shows for the first time in punishing weeks. The column still faces a caldera not yet finished with a slope that is not yet safe; there is nothing to do now but keep going.

The party works deeper into the valley edge, the first stubborn green breaking through grey and poisoned soil, and the way forward shrinks to one poor crossing with nothing better beside it. What stands between you and open ground is a rim undercut and liable to give way at the very edge. You gather the column, steady it with a word, and commit to the crossing.

A slow tongue of lava creeps across the foot of the greening slope and seals the line, and the wind takes the rim apart at your backs stone by stone. There is nothing left to do but pull the column back the long way it came, and so you do. You will be counting the cost of this hour for a long time to come, and there will be other lines, you tell yourself, if there is ever time to find them.

ending.title: Cut Off by the Lava Flow

nodes.body: You count the column into the waypoint in the far valley, fewer and slower than the last count; the far rim shows greener and gentler with every hard-won mile. The water casks ride light on the mules now, and every face around the fire is drawn thin with the crossing. You weigh the safe line, certain but punishing, against the faster ground the guide keeps eyeing with open hunger.

choices.label: Press on along the safe line.
Cut straight across the open ash to save a day.
Send a light party ahead to test the ground.
Push through despite the failing light.

nodes.body: You send a scout wide along the green rim while the column holds and waits. The detour costs the better part of an hour you can barely spare, but the loop earns its price, and the column gains a clearer picture of the miles ahead. The scout returns with the ground read and every trap flagged, and you bring the flanking party home to the column at the next waypoint along the safe line.

choices.label: Rejoin the column at the waypoint.

nodes.body: The line grinds forward over the far rim, moss holding improbably against the drifting ash. You are well off the safe line now, and it tells plainly in every wary, testing footfall; the guide warns of one careless step that could waste the whole crossing. The guide sets the pace and the column keeps it, you can almost taste clean water carried on the wind. Worse still, the line runs on toward a caldera not yet finished with a slope that is not yet safe; you count the party once more and keep going.

You drive the column further into the heart of the green rim, the first stubborn green breaking through grey and poisoned soil. The ground ahead narrows steadily to a single bad option and no other, and you set the guide at the front, take the rear yourself, and commit to the crossing.

You tally the stores at the far rim, and the tally is simply the end of the matter; there is not enough left to reach the valley and bring anyone home alive from it. You turn the column back while turning back can still be survived, and you will be counting the cost of this hour for a long time to come.

Another grinding stretch of the living margin passes underfoot, moss holding improbably against the drifting ash. Each mile off the known route trades a little more certainty for a little more speed. The guide warns of one careless step that could waste the whole crossing; the party presses on as living colour shows for the first time in punishing weeks. The column still faces a rim undercut and liable to give way at the very edge, and you steady the nearest shoulder with a word, and keep going.

The line grinds on into the green rim, ground softening at last toward growing, living things; the ground ahead narrows steadily to a single bad option and no other. The one line left runs you straight at one careless step that could waste the whole crossing. The party closes up tight, the air here runs a shade cleaner and steadies the column; there is nothing to gain by waiting, so you commit to the crossing.

Driven off the valley edge on every side by ground you cannot beat, you make the hardest call of the crossing. The whole expedition falls back toward base camp, mile by bitter mile, and you brought them out only to bring them home again. The valley is not lost, you tell them, only postponed; your people are alive, at least, and that will have to be enough for now, and you will be counting the cost of this hour for a long time to come.

You drive them another hard mile into the green rim, the first stubborn green breaking through grey and poisoned soil. You can feel the risk mounting under your boots, patient and indifferent. The guide warns of a caldera not yet finished with a slope that is not yet safe, and the only honest answer is forward, and you keep going.

Yet another sunless stretch of the far rim passes underfoot, the final hard climb before the valley proper opens. The margin for error thins the deeper you push past the safe line; the guide warns of a caldera not yet finished with a slope that is not yet safe. You can almost taste clean water carried on the wind, and the column knows fresh ash still drifting down to smother the new green may be waiting. Turning back would cost more than pressing on, so you keep going.

The party works deeper into the green rim, the final hard climb before the valley proper opens. There is no clever way through left, only the one grim crossing ahead; the guide warns of fresh ash still drifting down to smother the new green. The party closes up tight, you can almost taste clean water carried on the wind. There is no turning back now with the stores this low and the safe line a full day behind; there is nothing to gain by waiting, so you commit to the crossing.

The haze thickens over the green rim until you cannot make out the head of your own column. The guide loses the line and admits as much, and there is no shame in the honest admitting of it. You feel your way back toward known ground, the valley given up for another, clearer day, and you will be counting the cost of this hour for a long time to come.

You count the column into the waypoint in the far valley, fewer and slower than the last count. The ground begins, at long last, to fall away toward green, and the stores will not survive a squandered day, and every idle hour costs one you cannot spare. Speed and safety pull against each other in your hands, and the whole column hangs on which you choose. The riskier the ground, the more it demands of a column already scraped this close to empty.

choices.label: Press on along the safe line.
Scout a flanking line before moving up.
Cut straight across the open ash to save a day.
Send a light party ahead to test the ground.
Push through despite the failing light.

nodes.body: The line grinds forward over the greening slope, ground softening at last toward growing, living things; you can feel the risk mounting under your boots, patient and indifferent. The guide warns of one careless step that could waste the whole crossing, and you can almost taste clean water carried on the wind. Worse still, the line runs on toward fresh ash still drifting down to smother the new green; you set your jaw against the doubt and keep going.

The party works deeper into the far rim, ground softening at last toward growing, living things. The ground ahead narrows steadily to a single bad option and no other, and the crossing means facing fresh ash still drifting down to smother the new green. The party closes up tight, you can almost taste clean water carried on the wind. You have spent far too much ground to wheel the column around at this hour, and there is only one line left to take, so you commit to the crossing.

On the far rim the column's nerve gives out all at once and all together. They will not take a single further step toward the smoking pit, and saying so plainly costs them nothing now; you swallow the loss whole and turn the entire exhausted party back. The valley stays exactly where it was, a green rumour on the far side of the ash, and the caldera does not care in the least that you tried.

ending.title: The Party Refuses to Go On

nodes.body: You drive them another hard mile into the living margin, the first stubborn green breaking through grey and poisoned soil. The margin for error thins the deeper you push past the safe line. The ground ahead promises fresh ash still drifting down to smother the new green, and the only honest answer is forward, and you keep going.

You press on where the green rim closes tighter around the column, cinder giving way, grudgingly, to root and stem; there is no clever way through left, only the one grim crossing ahead. What stands between you and open ground is a rim undercut and liable to give way at the very edge. The air here runs a shade cleaner and steadies the column; retreat from here would break what little is left of the party's strength and nerve. You give the word the party has been dreading and commit to the crossing.

The ground gives way wet and hungry beneath the labouring column somewhere out on the greening slope, and packs and casks go down into the boiling ooze faster than desperate hands can drag them clear. You pull every soul back onto firm footing, poorer by half and no nearer the valley for any of it. The valley stays exactly where it was, a green rumour on the far side of the ash.

You drive them another hard mile into the green rim, the final hard climb before the valley proper opens. The further the party strays from the proven line, the higher the stakes climb on every step. The ground ahead promises a rim undercut and liable to give way at the very edge. The party presses on as you can almost taste clean water carried on the wind; the only honest answer is forward, and you keep going.

You push the spent party on across the greening slope, cinder giving way, grudgingly, to root and stem. The safe line is a memory now, and the ground out here keeps no such promises. The danger here is a rim undercut and liable to give way at the very edge; retreat solves nothing out here, so you keep going.

The party works deeper into the valley edge, the first stubborn green breaking through grey and poisoned soil. The route pinches down until a single dangerous line is all that is on offer; the danger on this line is one careless step that could waste the whole crossing. The guide reads a caldera not yet finished with a slope that is not yet safe and says nothing you want to hear. You have spent far too much ground to wheel the column around at this hour; you set the guide at the front, take the rear yourself, and commit to the crossing.

A lava tube runs hollow beneath the line here on the green rim, and its roof lets go in a single grinding roar of falling stone. You get every soul back clear of the collapse before the dust has even begun to settle. The way ahead is shut now, an hour of digging or a week of it, and you have neither hour nor week to give; you will be counting the cost of this hour for a long time to come.

The waypoint in the far valley rises out of the murk, and the column staggers gratefully into it. The ground begins, at long last, to fall away toward green. The crossing has taken something from every one of them, and the far half still waits. You turn the same question over again, the patient road against the hungry shortcut; every shortcut across this wasteland has already buried someone who trusted it.

choices.label: Press on toward the far valley.
Cut straight across the open ash to save a day.
Send a light party ahead to test the ground.
Push through despite the failing light.

nodes.body: The column drags itself up the last of the ridge pass and halts as one exhausted body; there, far below and well beyond the grey, lies the green rim of the last living valley. After six legs of ash and dead ground the sight of living colour goes through the party like a struck note. You let them look for one long breath, then turn them toward the descent and press on to the valley.

The way down to the valley floor is choked with ash still falling grey and steady out of the sky. You pass the last of the water hand to hand along the line, a single mouthful for each and no more; the caldera has chosen this hour to shed its worst, and the column goes down half-blind through the drift. There is no waiting this out and no water left to wait with, so you order the final push to the valley.

A tongue of fresh lava has crawled across the approach and split the only way forward in two. You throw charred timber across the narrowest place and send the column over it one careful soul at a time. The caldera booms close behind you now, urging the crossing on faster than is anywhere near safe; the last plank holds, the last soul steps clear of the glow, and you press on to the valley.

The valley gate stands open across one last wide field of settling grey cinder; the whole expedition is still with you, footsore and grey to the eyes but every one of them whole. You can count every soul who left base camp, and every one of them can see the green ahead. One field of ash remains between the column and the valley, and you press steadily on across it.

At the very threshold of the valley you halt the column and count heads one final, careful time. Every soul is accounted for, from the guide at the head of the line to the rear guard limping in last. The last line lies just ahead, and how you take the column across it is the final choice of the whole crossing. You can lead them all across together, split the party to force a faster crossing, or send scouts first and hold the column back.

choices.label: Lead the whole expedition across together.
Split the party to force a faster crossing.
Send scouts first and hold the column back.

nodes.body: You lead the whole expedition across the last line together, and together it walks down into the valley. Behind you the caldera finally lets go, blowing the pass shut in a roaring column of fire and stone and sealing the wasteland away for good. It comes far too late to catch you, and every soul who mustered at base camp now stands on living ground. There is green water at their feet and clean air in their aching lungs, and you have brought every one of them home.

ending.title: The Whole Expedition Reaches the Valley

nodes.body: You split the party to force the pace, and the hard gamble carries the crossing. The forward group reaches the valley clean, but the rear guard loses its stores to the ash and limps in long after, empty-handed and grey to the bone. Everyone lives, and that much at least you held onto, though the cost of it is written plain on every hollowed face. The expedition survives in the valley, battered and whole and far quieter than it was, and you let yourself sit down at last.

You send the scouts ahead and hold the column back on the rim until their signal comes. They find the one safe descent in the failing light, and the column follows it down by lantern, roped together and painfully slow. It takes the better part of the night, but the expedition reaches the valley floor intact, not one soul lost to the dark. You come last across the line, as a captain always should, counting each of them safe before yourself, and then you step off the ash onto living ground.
